2: Current agriculture practices are considered among the most important drivers in the degradation of land and freshwater (Foley et al., 2011), and of ecosystem services (Fagerholm et al., 2016). Moreover, agriculture is responsible for a large part of the global anthropogenic GHG emissions, and the livestock sector in partic- ular is responsible for 18% of these emissions (FAO, 2006), or possibly even more (Goodland and Anhang, 2009; Herrero et al., 2011). Reducing the environmental impact of animal rearing is unlikely in the future, given the population growth and the consequent increase in food demand, and meat consumption in particular. A current challenge of modern agriculture is therefore to combine high productivity with a low impact, in order to feed the growing world population while increasing agriculture's
sustainability. Achieving such a goal is one of the greatest current scientific challenges (Tilman et al., 2002).

Chicken meat has lower environmental impact than other meats (Williams et al., 2006), due to the high feed conversion efficiency. However, the extreme selection for high growth rate and feed conversion efficiency negatively affected the health status and behavior of the chickens (Bokkers and Koene, 2003; Turner et al., 2003), favoring skeletal imbalances (Kestin et al., 1992, 2001; Corr et al., 2003a, b) and other metabolic and muscle disorders (Branciari et al., 2009, 2014). Animal welfare and health are major concerns in intensive poultry systems (EU, 2000). Organic and free- range production systems have been developed in response to such problems (EU, 1999).

Organic and free-range chickens generally use slow-growing strains (Moyle et al., 2014; Sirri et al., 2011), which show better health and animal welfare (Castellini et al., 2008) and produce meat of higher quality (Castellini et al., 2006b Sirri et al., 2011; Sossidou et al., 2015; Dal Bosco et al., 2016) in comparison to conventional intensive system.

Despite the many benefits, free-range systems need grazing space, and this increases the use of land. In fact, grazing provides bioactive compounds that increase meat quality (Fanatico et al., 2006), but does not supply much energy and proteins for the birds' diets (Walker and Gordon, 2003; Buchanan et al., 2007; Rivera-Ferre et al., 2007). Therefore, the additional land use due to grazing in free-range systems is not compensated for by a pro- portional reduction in land use for feed production.

One possible way to reduce land use for grazing is to let the animals graze in an orchard, where the land is already used for the trees and the animals do not require any additional land. Chickens benefit from trees in terms of protection from predators, sun, wind, and extreme temperatures. As a result, chickens spend more time grazing, ingesting larger amounts of forage and bioactive com- pounds (Bubier and Bradshaw, 1998; Mirabito and Lubac, 2001; Dal Bosco et al., 2014a). In addition, combining free-range animals with orchards provides other environmental benefits by providing weeding and fertilization to the trees, thus reducing the impact of the olive orchard to almost zero, except for the land use (Paolotti et al., 2016). Similar results were found also using geese instead of poultry, applying an emergy approach (Patrizi et al., 2017).

The comparison between different rearing systems is appropriate only when the output/externality of the systems (environment, health, welfare, product quality, etc.) are homogeneous. On the contrary, many authors recognize benefits of free-range systems in terms of bird welfare and health (Castellini et al., 2006a), meat quality (Chartrin et al., 2005; Fanatico et al., 2005a,b; Lewis et al., 1997; Sirri et al., 2010), landscape aesthetics and biodiversity (Mugnai et al., 2009). These benefits should be considered with a more comprehensive model, such as the Multicriteria Decision Analysis (MCDA), a family of methods (Greco et al., 2016), which allows to consider several opposite criteria at the same time and also the points of view of different stakeholders (Kadzinski et al., 2017).

Several authors focused on the comparison of different rearing systems. Most studies compared the environmental impact of free- range VS. intensive systems (Veleva et al., 2001; Tilman et al., 2002; Castellini et al., 2006a, 2012b; Bokker and De Boer, 2009; Boggia et al., 2010; Cerutti et al., 2011; Acosta-Alba et al., 2012; Lindsey, 2012; Zhang et al., 2012). None of these studies applied a multi- criteria approach. Castellini et al. (2012a) used the multicriteria approach for comparing a conventional intensive system with organic and "organic plus" system, but the combination with an orchard was not considered. Only one previous study (Paolotti et al., 2016) considered poultry systems combined with an or- chard, but did not include a multicriteria approach, nor an intensive system.

In the present study, therefore, we compared the global impact of intensive, free-range, and free-range combined (with an olive orchard) systems, using a MCDA analysis, according to a sustain- ability approach, which included environmental, economic and social criteria, reflecting a 'triple bottom line' (TBL) approach to sustainability assessment (Pope et al., 2004). Such criteria were used for developing a MCDA model based on the PROMETHEE methods. This is the first study evaluating the overall sustainability of an integrated livestock-orchard system, compared to a non- integrated free-range system and an intensive system, using a MCDA approach.

This is the first study evaluating the overall sustainability of an integrated livestock-orchard system, compared to a non- integrated free-range system and an intensive system, using a MCDA approach.
7: Current agriculture practices are considered among the most important drivers in the degradation of land and freshwater (Foley et al., 2011), and of ecosystem services (Fagerholm et al., 2016). Moreover, agriculture is responsible for a large part of the global anthropogenic GHG emissions, and the livestock sector in partic- ular is responsible for 18% of these emissions (FAO, 2006), or possibly even more (Goodland and Anhang, 2009; Herrero et al., 2011). Reducing the environmental impact of animal rearing is unlikely in the future, given the population growth and the consequent increase in food demand, and meat consumption in particular. A current challenge of modern agriculture is therefore to combine high productivity with a low impact, in order to feed the growing world population while increasing agriculture's
sustainability. Achieving such a goal is one of the greatest current scientific challenges (Tilman et al., 2002).

Chicken meat has lower environmental impact than other meats (Williams et al., 2006), due to the high feed conversion efficiency. However, the extreme selection for high growth rate and feed conversion efficiency negatively affected the health status and behavior of the chickens (Bokkers and Koene, 2003; Turner et al., 2003), favoring skeletal imbalances (Kestin et al., 1992, 2001; Corr et al., 2003a, b) and other metabolic and muscle disorders (Branciari et al., 2009, 2014). Animal welfare and health are major concerns in intensive poultry systems (EU, 2000). Organic and free- range production systems have been developed in response to such problems (EU, 1999).

Organic and free-range chickens generally use slow-growing strains (Moyle et al., 2014; Sirri et al., 2011), which show better health and animal welfare (Castellini et al., 2008) and produce meat of higher quality (Castellini et al., 2006b Sirri et al., 2011; Sossidou et al., 2015; Dal Bosco et al., 2016) in comparison to conventional intensive system.

Despite the many benefits, free-range systems need grazing space, and this increases the use of land. In fact, grazing provides bioactive compounds that increase meat quality (Fanatico et al., 2006), but does not supply much energy and proteins for the birds' diets (Walker and Gordon, 2003; Buchanan et al., 2007; Rivera-Ferre et al., 2007). Therefore, the additional land use due to grazing in free-range systems is not compensated for by a pro- portional reduction in land use for feed production.

One possible way to reduce land use for grazing is to let the animals graze in an orchard, where the land is already used for the trees and the animals do not require any additional land. Chickens benefit from trees in terms of protection from predators, sun, wind, and extreme temperatures. As a result, chickens spend more time grazing, ingesting larger amounts of forage and bioactive com- pounds (Bubier and Bradshaw, 1998; Mirabito and Lubac, 2001; Dal Bosco et al., 2014a). In addition, combining free-range animals with orchards provides other environmental benefits by providing weeding and fertilization to the trees, thus reducing the impact of the olive orchard to almost zero, except for the land use (Paolotti et al., 2016). Similar results were found also using geese instead of poultry, applying an emergy approach (Patrizi et al., 2017).

The comparison between different rearing systems is appropriate only when the output/externality of the systems (environment, health, welfare, product quality, etc.) are homogeneous. On the contrary, many authors recognize benefits of free-range systems in terms of bird welfare and health (Castellini et al., 2006a), meat quality (Chartrin et al., 2005; Fanatico et al., 2005a,b; Lewis et al., 1997; Sirri et al., 2010), landscape aesthetics and biodiversity (Mugnai et al., 2009). These benefits should be considered with a more comprehensive model, such as the Multicriteria Decision Analysis (MCDA), a family of methods (Greco et al., 2016), which allows to consider several opposite criteria at the same time and also the points of view of different stakeholders (Kadzinski et al., 2017).

Several authors focused on the comparison of different rearing systems. Most studies compared the environmental impact of free- range VS. intensive systems (Veleva et al., 2001; Tilman et al., 2002; Castellini et al., 2006a, 2012b; Bokker and De Boer, 2009; Boggia et al., 2010; Cerutti et al., 2011; Acosta-Alba et al., 2012; Lindsey, 2012; Zhang et al., 2012). None of these studies applied a multi- criteria approach. Castellini et al. (2012a) used the multicriteria approach for comparing a conventional intensive system with organic and "organic plus" system, but the combination with an orchard was not considered. Only one previous study (Paolotti et al., 2016) considered poultry systems combined with an or- chard, but did not include a multicriteria approach, nor an intensive system.

In the present study, therefore, we compared the global impact of intensive, free-range, and free-range combined (with an olive orchard) systems, using a MCDA analysis, according to a sustain- ability approach, which included environmental, economic and social criteria, reflecting a 'triple bottom line' (TBL) approach to sustainability assessment (Pope et al., 2004). Such criteria were used for developing a MCDA model based on the PROMETHEE methods. This is the first study evaluating the overall sustainability of an integrated livestock-orchard system, compared to a non- integrated free-range system and an intensive system, using a MCDA approach.

9: The main steps of the methodology followed in this study were: i) the analysis and data collection of three different poultry systems (intensive, free-range and free-range combined with an olive or- chard), ii) the selection of relevant environmental, social and eco- nomic criteria needed to compare the level of sustainability of the

three systems; in fact, in order to compare the three systems with the multicriteria analysis, a set of different criteria, divided in three groups (environmental, social, economic) were selected, iii) the application of a multicriteria method to achieve a final ranking, including a standardization and weighting procedure, based on three different panels of stakeholders: farmers, consumers and scientists.
10: Three different poultry systems were analyzed and then compared: a conventional, intensive system, a free-range system, and a free-range system inside an olive orchard, that we called "combined" system. We chose the system characteristics corre- sponding to current commercial intensive and free-range poultry systems in central Italy (Table 1).

The intensive system was represented by traditional broiler rearing, using meat-type birds, concentrated feed and controlled housing (artificial light and climate control, automatic water and feed supply). The animal concentration was 1.000 birds per cycle, with 6.4 cycles per year (12.69 birds per Cycle length was 57 days.

In both the free-range and combined systems animal density was 1.000 birds per hectare per cycle (10 m² of pasture per bird). Cycle length was approximately 90 days. We assumed three cycles per year with a fallow period between cycles for sanitation. Both systems had a mobile housing just for the night, consisting of very simple shelters, with a light structure (steel and wood) made with local materials, and they were not considered in the analysis.

The free-range poultry system was reared in a traditional pastureland, with birds grazing on land used only for grazing, implying a land use impact of the poultry system. In the combined system, instead, birds grazed in an olive orchard; in this case, the land is already used for the orchard, therefore the grazing has no additional land use impact. Moreover, in the combined system the impact of mechanical mowing and chemical fertilization of the orchard is eliminated, because the chickens provide these services naturally in the orchard Therefore, combining one hectare of olive orchard with one hectare of free range chickens provides benefits for both of the systems: the orchard, when grazed, and the poultry system, when pastureland is provided by the orchard (Paolotti et al., 2016).

12: Environmental criteria were addressed using the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach and a biodiversity index.
13: 2.2.1.1. Goal and scope definition in the LCA study, including func- tional unit and system boundaries. A life cycle assessment (LCA) was performed for the three systems, according to the procedure
Table 1 Characteristics of the poultry systems analyzed.

Characteristics|Intensive|Free-range|Combined Genetic strain used|Fast-growing|Slow-growing|Slow-growing Total birds per cycle (N)|1000|1000|1000 Cycles of production (n/year)|6.4|3.0|3.0 Building area (m ²|80|-|- Density (birds/m²|12.69|0.1|0.1 Pasture area (ha)||1|1 Feed conversion|1.9|3.3|3.3 Final live weight (kg)|2.6|2.8|2.8 Meat produced (t/year)|16.6|8.4|8.4
a kg of feed per kg of animal live weight.

established by the standards ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 (2006a,b), which set four basic phases for this kind of studies: Goal and Scope definition, Life Cycle Inventory, Life Cycle Assessment and Inter- pretation. The LCA method enables the environmental implications of a product/process/service to be analyzed throughout the stages in its life cycle, via the quantification of the use of the resources (energy, raw materials, water) and of the emissions into the envi- ronment (emissions into the air, water and soil, waste) and co- products associated with the system under evaluation.

The main phases analyzed in the LCA studies went from the production of the initial necessary inputs (cultivation of the main feed ingredients) to the rearing phase (production of poultry), including the intermediate feed manufacturing and transport processes, and omitting the final product distribution, which was the same for the three systems. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries and the main phases and related processes considered for the three case studies. The functional unit chosen for the study, which is the reference unit for the calculation of cash inflows and out flows from the system (Paolotti et al., 2017), was 1 ton of poultry meat.

2.2.1.2. Inventory analysis and information on data used and as- sumptions made in the study. Concerning the main data of the intensive system, the IPCC BREF average values (European Commission, 2017) were used, about energy and water (for clean- ing and for birds) consumption, and NH3 emissions coming from manure, and bird housing. Other types of emissions, like methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N20) and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOC) can be considered very low within intensive systems with high frequencies of manure removal (European Commission, 2017). For this reason, they were not considered in the study. The Regional Regulation for effluent spreading (n. 1492/ 2006) was used as a reference for the amount of manure produced and the associated N emissions in the soil. For the free-range and combined systems, the same data sources were used, but in this cases energy consumption and water for cleaning the birds'
Fig. 1. System boundaries and the main phases and related processes considered for the three case studies.
housing were not present, while NH3 emissions from the small and temporary shelters was probably negligible and not considered.

The data collection related to the cultivation of the main feed ingredients (i.e. maize, soybean, fava bean and wheat, including the agricultural operations involved), and to the processing of crops into feed was the same for the three systems analyzed. Regarding the cultivation of the main feed ingredients, the FADN Database (Farm Accountancy Data Network, 2013) was consulted, for regional data related to the investigated area, together with in- terviews with local experts. About the transformation process from crops to feed, a local plant provided information about electricity and gas consumption, quantity of material that is generally pro- cessed in one year, and emissions of dust, nitrate and sulfur oxides, connected to the transformation process (Paolotti et al., 2016).

The free range poultry rearing system studied represented the one adopted locally by a registered consortium of stakeholders (Pollo Rurale Umbria: www.pollorurale.it), following a precise protocol of rearing from which we derived bird density, cycle length and chicken breed. Other general data (background data about transport, fuel consumption, generic materials production and electricity) involved in all the phases of the life cycles, were taken from the Ecoinvent database 2.2 (Ecoinvent, 2010).

The rearing infrastructures were not considered in the three LCA studies, because capital goods are often not included (Goedkoop et al., 2013) and, in the case of free-range and combined, also because the shelters (only for the night) have a negligible impact. Capital goods were included for background data, and mainly excluded for foreground data (Goedkoop et al., 2013).

Finally, in the LCA of the combined system, data coincide with those of the free-range system, but the environmental advantages related to the omitted mowing and fertilization practices, including the omitted production of fertilizers, were also computed, consid- ering them as "avoided products" (Goedkoop et al., 2013). An "avoided product" is when product X is replaced by product Y, therefore the environmental load of product X is avoided and can be subtracted from product Y's environmental load (Goedkoop et al., 2013). In the combined system scenario, the orchard would necessitate considering the processes of mechanical mowing and chemical fertilization, including the production of fertilizers, ac- cording to the life cycle approach of considering the production of raw materials. As the birds provide "natural" weeding and fertil- ization, these processes are avoided in the orchard. Practically, this means that the impact of these processes and operations were subtracted from the total impacts of the combined system, which is otherwise identical to the other free range system.

2.2.1.3. Impact assessment phase. For the multicriteria analysis, five of the eleven categories calculated applying Eco-Indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001) were chosen. The Eco- Indicator 99 method was chosen as it is one of the most widely used in LCA studies, and as it is based on "endpoint models", allowing the consideration of a so-called "damage-oriented approach" (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001; Paolotti et al., 2015). In order to choose the categories with the greatest environmental impact an LCA normalization procedure was applied. This LCA normalization process identifies the relative significance of each category (Kim et al., 2012). Climate Change, Respiratory Inorganics, Acidification/Eutrophication, Land Use, and Fossil Fuels were

1 Normalization consists of dividing the impact category indicators by a 'normal' value. The most common procedure to obtain a normal value is to determine the impact category indicators for a region during an entire year and divide this value by the number of inhabitants in that area. Therefore, the final results are expressed in points; the higher the score, the more important the impact is.
identified as the most important categories (Fig. 2), therefore we considered only these 5 categories in the MCDA. Table 2 reports the meaning of these categories.
The same phases were considered for the three systems, intensive, free-range and combined.

To measure the biodiversity associated with each system, a combination of two different indices were used. The former index used was proposed by Castellini et al. (2012a) and refers to the genetic diversity of reared birds while the latter is a naturalness index, proposed by Machado (2004), which categorizes the envi- ronment and its suitability for natural life. The two indices have been calculated for the different systems separately, using the ap- proaches described in the mentioned studies, and then summed. The value of the two sub-indices and the results used in the present application are reported in Table 3.

Regarding the cultivation of the main feed ingredients, the FADN Database (Farm Accountancy Data Network, 2013) was consulted, for regional data related to the investigated area, together with in- terviews with local experts. About the transformation process from crops to feed, a local plant provided information about electricity and gas consumption, quantity of material that is generally pro- cessed in one year, and emissions of dust, nitrate and sulfur oxides, connected to the transformation process (Paolotti et al., 2016).

The free range poultry rearing system studied represented the one adopted locally by a registered consortium of stakeholders (Pollo Rurale Umbria: www.pollorurale.it), following a precise protocol of rearing from which we derived bird density, cycle length and chicken breed. Other general data (background data about transport, fuel consumption, generic materials production and electricity) involved in all the phases of the life cycles, were taken from the Ecoinvent database 2.2 (Ecoinvent, 2010).

The rearing infrastructures were not considered in the three LCA studies, because capital goods are often not included (Goedkoop et al., 2013) and, in the case of free-range and combined, also because the shelters (only for the night) have a negligible impact. Capital goods were included for background data, and mainly excluded for foreground data (Goedkoop et al., 2013).

Finally, in the LCA of the combined system, data coincide with those of the free-range system, but the environmental advantages related to the omitted mowing and fertilization practices, including the omitted production of fertilizers, were also computed, consid- ering them as "avoided products" (Goedkoop et al., 2013). An "avoided product" is when product X is replaced by product Y, therefore the environmental load of product X is avoided and can be subtracted from product Y's environmental load (Goedkoop et al., 2013). In the combined system scenario, the orchard would necessitate considering the processes of mechanical mowing and chemical fertilization, including the production of fertilizers, ac- cording to the life cycle approach of considering the production of raw materials. As the birds provide "natural" weeding and fertil- ization, these processes are avoided in the orchard. Practically, this means that the impact of these processes and operations were subtracted from the total impacts of the combined system, which is otherwise identical to the other free range system.

2.2.1.3. Impact assessment phase. For the multicriteria analysis, five of the eleven categories calculated applying Eco-Indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001) were chosen. The Eco- Indicator 99 method was chosen as it is one of the most widely used in LCA studies, and as it is based on "endpoint models", allowing the consideration of a so-called "damage-oriented approach" (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001; Paolotti et al., 2015). In order to choose the categories with the greatest environmental impact an LCA normalization procedure was applied. This LCA normalization process identifies the relative significance of each category (Kim et al., 2012). Climate Change, Respiratory Inorganics, Acidification/Eutrophication, Land Use, and Fossil Fuels were

1 Normalization consists of dividing the impact category indicators by a 'normal' value. The most common procedure to obtain a normal value is to determine the impact category indicators for a region during an entire year and divide this value by the number of inhabitants in that area. Therefore, the final results are expressed in points; the higher the score, the more important the impact is.
identified as the most important categories (Fig. 2), therefore we considered only these 5 categories in the MCDA. Table 2 reports the meaning of these categories.
The same phases were considered for the three systems, intensive, free-range and combined.

To measure the biodiversity associated with each system, a combination of two different indices were used. The former index used was proposed by Castellini et al. (2012a) and refers to the genetic diversity of reared birds while the latter is a naturalness index, proposed by Machado (2004), which categorizes the envi- ronment and its suitability for natural life. The two indices have been calculated for the different systems separately, using the ap- proaches described in the mentioned studies, and then summed. The value of the two sub-indices and the results used in the present application are reported in Table 3.

To measure the biodiversity associated with each system, a combination of two different indices were used. The former index used was proposed by Castellini et al. (2012a) and refers to the genetic diversity of reared birds while the latter is a naturalness index, proposed by Machado (2004), which categorizes the envi- ronment and its suitability for natural life. The two indices have been calculated for the different systems separately, using the ap- proaches described in the mentioned studies, and then summed. The value of the two sub-indices and the results used in the present application are reported in Table 3.
20: 2.2.1.1. Goal and scope definition in the LCA study, including func- tional unit and system boundaries. A life cycle assessment (LCA) was performed for the three systems, according to the procedure

established by the standards ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 (2006a,b), which set four basic phases for this kind of studies: Goal and Scope definition, Life Cycle Inventory, Life Cycle Assessment and Inter- pretation. The LCA method enables the environmental implications of a product/process/service to be analyzed throughout the stages in its life cycle, via the quantification of the use of the resources (energy, raw materials, water) and of the emissions into the envi- ronment (emissions into the air, water and soil, waste) and co- products associated with the system under evaluation.

The main phases analyzed in the LCA studies went from the production of the initial necessary inputs (cultivation of the main feed ingredients) to the rearing phase (production of poultry), including the intermediate feed manufacturing and transport processes, and omitting the final product distribution, which was the same for the three systems. Fig. 1 shows the system boundaries and the main phases and related processes considered for the three case studies. The functional unit chosen for the study, which is the reference unit for the calculation of cash inflows and out flows from the system (Paolotti et al., 2017), was 1 ton of poultry meat.

2.2.1.2. Inventory analysis and information on data used and as- sumptions made in the study. Concerning the main data of the intensive system, the IPCC BREF average values (European Commission, 2017) were used, about energy and water (for clean- ing and for birds) consumption, and NH3 emissions coming from manure, and bird housing. Other types of emissions, like methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N20) and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOC) can be considered very low within intensive systems with high frequencies of manure removal (European Commission, 2017). For this reason, they were not considered in the study. The Regional Regulation for effluent spreading (n. 1492/ 2006) was used as a reference for the amount of manure produced and the associated N emissions in the soil. For the free-range and combined systems, the same data sources were used, but in this cases energy consumption and water for cleaning the birds'
Fig. 1. System boundaries and the main phases and related processes considered for the three case studies.
housing were not present, while NH3 emissions from the small and temporary shelters was probably negligible and not considered.

The data collection related to the cultivation of the main feed ingredients (i.e. maize, soybean, fava bean and wheat, including the agricultural operations involved), and to the processing of crops into feed was the same for the three systems analyzed. Regarding the cultivation of the main feed ingredients, the FADN Database (Farm Accountancy Data Network, 2013) was consulted, for regional data related to the investigated area, together with in- terviews with local experts. About the transformation process from crops to feed, a local plant provided information about electricity and gas consumption, quantity of material that is generally pro- cessed in one year, and emissions of dust, nitrate and sulfur oxides, connected to the transformation process (Paolotti et al., 2016).

The free range poultry rearing system studied represented the one adopted locally by a registered consortium of stakeholders (Pollo Rurale Umbria: www.pollorurale.it), following a precise protocol of rearing from which we derived bird density, cycle length and chicken breed. Other general data (background data about transport, fuel consumption, generic materials production and electricity) involved in all the phases of the life cycles, were taken from the Ecoinvent database 2.2 (Ecoinvent, 2010).

The rearing infrastructures were not considered in the three LCA studies, because capital goods are often not included (Goedkoop et al., 2013) and, in the case of free-range and combined, also because the shelters (only for the night) have a negligible impact. Capital goods were included for background data, and mainly excluded for foreground data (Goedkoop et al., 2013).

Finally, in the LCA of the combined system, data coincide with those of the free-range system, but the environmental advantages related to the omitted mowing and fertilization practices, including the omitted production of fertilizers, were also computed, consid- ering them as "avoided products" (Goedkoop et al., 2013). An "avoided product" is when product X is replaced by product Y, therefore the environmental load of product X is avoided and can be subtracted from product Y's environmental load (Goedkoop et al., 2013). In the combined system scenario, the orchard would necessitate considering the processes of mechanical mowing and chemical fertilization, including the production of fertilizers, ac- cording to the life cycle approach of considering the production of raw materials. As the birds provide "natural" weeding and fertil- ization, these processes are avoided in the orchard. Practically, this means that the impact of these processes and operations were subtracted from the total impacts of the combined system, which is otherwise identical to the other free range system.

2.2.1.3. Impact assessment phase. For the multicriteria analysis, five of the eleven categories calculated applying Eco-Indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001) were chosen. The Eco- Indicator 99 method was chosen as it is one of the most widely used in LCA studies, and as it is based on "endpoint models", allowing the consideration of a so-called "damage-oriented approach" (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001; Paolotti et al., 2015). In order to choose the categories with the greatest environmental impact an LCA normalization procedure was applied. This LCA normalization process identifies the relative significance of each category (Kim et al., 2012). Climate Change, Respiratory Inorganics, Acidification/Eutrophication, Land Use, and Fossil Fuels were

1 Normalization consists of dividing the impact category indicators by a 'normal' value. The most common procedure to obtain a normal value is to determine the impact category indicators for a region during an entire year and divide this value by the number of inhabitants in that area. Therefore, the final results are expressed in points; the higher the score, the more important the impact is.
identified as the most important categories (Fig. 2), therefore we considered only these 5 categories in the MCDA. Table 2 reports the meaning of these categories.
The same phases were considered for the three systems, intensive, free-range and combined.

To measure the biodiversity associated with each system, a combination of two different indices were used. The former index used was proposed by Castellini et al. (2012a) and refers to the genetic diversity of reared birds while the latter is a naturalness index, proposed by Machado (2004), which categorizes the envi- ronment and its suitability for natural life. The two indices have been calculated for the different systems separately, using the ap- proaches described in the mentioned studies, and then summed. The value of the two sub-indices and the results used in the present application are reported in Table 3.
21: Social criteria were divided into three sets of criteria: labor safety, animal welfare and landscape. The first two criteria were chosen according to the paper of Castellini et al. (2012a,b). The inclusion of landscape is due to the increasing expectation of so- ciety for it, in a context of undergoing complex processes as emerging by literature (Pinto-Correia and Kristensen, 2013).

23: Consumers are very concerned about the way animals are treated and cared for during their life time (Van Asselt et al., 2015). Therefore, we included some criteria on animal welfare. In partic- ular, we considered behavioral observation, space, outdoor time and health of the poultry.

The behavioral observations included the following activities: moving (running, walking, foraging, and stretching), rest (lying and standing), eating (food and water), ground pecking, comfort and others (social behaviors). Among all of the behaviors recorded, only moving activity was considered in the MCDA because this was the most characterizing behavior of the different systems. We also considered stocking density, time spending outdoor, breast blister and foot pad lesions. Data for these criteria were taken from pre- vious studies (Castellini et al., 2012a; Singh et al., 2009; Kjaer et al., 2006; Dal Bosco et al., 2014a,b) and are shown in Table 6.
24: In developed countries, consumers are more concerned about farming and food provisioning issues, including the landscape (Weatherell et al., 2003), which has a main rule in territorial sus- tainability also because of its scenic and recreational value (Fagioli et al., 2017). Natural amenity, habitat, landscape and animal welfare are related non-market benefits, and the visual impact of each production system appeals differently to the population (Smith et al., 2012). Therefore, an arbitrary index based on the visual impact of the three systems analyzed was used, based on the methodology proposed by Rodriguez-Loinaz et al. (2015) to consider the impact of landscape on the society, according to the different systems of production. The index considered how much each system is integrated in the typical landscape of central Italy, characterized by a broad presence of olive orchards. Data are shown in Table 6.
25: Economic criteria were divided into three sets of criteria: clas- sical economic criteria, animal performance criteria and meat quality criteria.
26: To evaluate the economics returns of the three production sys- items, the net income per kilo (€/kg), which is the difference be- tween gross production and all the costs (both fixed and variable), was estimated using data from farms in central Italy (Table 4). For the combined system, we assessed the costs of fertilization and mowing in the orchard, since these operations are unnecessary when the poultry are present. These avoided costs in the orchard can be considered as extra benefits provided by the chickens. For the three systems, the most important cost is the feed, which represents around 60% of the total costs for the intensive and about 70% for the other two systems.
27: Animal performances criteria affect indirectly the economic results. Thus, we considered two performance criteria for the different production systems (Castellini et al., 2012a,b). The criteria are: feed conversion, which measures the consumption of feed per kilogram of meat, and mortality rate, expressed as the number of

31: <header>2.5. Multicriteria analysis </header>

To rank the three poultry systems, all the criteria described in section 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 were processed using PROMETHEE (Brans, 1982; Vincke and Brans, 1985), one of the most used outranking methods in the Multicriteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) family, applied to several fields of research (Behzadian et al., 2010). In particular, both PROMETHEE I (partial ranking) and PROMETHEE II (complete ranking) were applied, using Visual PROMETHEE software.

The structure preference of PROMETHEE is based on a pairwise comparison of two alternatives, for each criterion, that have to be maximized or minimized. The deviation between the evaluations of two alternatives on each criterion is considered: the larger the deviation, the larger the preference for one alternative.

Fig. 3 shows the stepwise procedure for applying PROMETHEE I and II (Behzadian et al., 2010), as applied also in this work. The first step is the determination of the deviation based on the pairwise comparison. In step 2, a preference function is applied to each criterion, to calculate the global preference index in the further Step 3. In our application, a linear preference function was applied to all the criteria except Labor Safety index, for which a level function was adopted. To estimate the global preference index, a weighting process is required. In outranking methods, as the PROMETHEE, weights represent the degree of importance of criteria, not considering the trade-off and scaling effects (de Almeida Filho e al., 2018). PROMETHEE assumes the ability of Decision Makers to weight the criteria appropriately (Behzadian et al., 2010; Macharis et al., 2004). Since several studies proposed the use of ordinal in- formation to establish weights values (de Almeida Filho e al., 2018), the weighting phase was processed by classifying the effects in priority categories.
Three different groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists), were asked to assign a priority to the different criteria (1,

first position: the most important; 3, last position: the least important). Ten Italian poultry scientists, 15 consumers and 15 farmers were interviewed and the mean values were reported. All stakeholders were informed about the meaning of each criterion to be sure that they properly classified the criteria. The results of such process are reported in Table 5 and were used to calculate the weights, which were then used in the MCDA.

Finally, in step 4, the positive and negative outranking flows were calculated for each alternative. The positive outranking flow (+(a)) represents the power of outranking of an alternative over all the others: the higher the +(a) the better the alternative (Brans and De Smet, 2016). The negative outranking flow (O -(a)) is a measure of the weakness of an alternative, and of how much it is outranked by all the others: the smaller the (a) the better the alternative (Brans and De Smet, 2016). PROMETHEE II goes to step 5 and calculates the net outranking flow (a), which is a measure of the balance between positive and negative flows. The higher the net flow, the better the alternative. However, in real-world appli- cations it is recommended to apply both PROMETHEE I and II: the complete ranking is easier to use, but the partial one can be useful to finalize a proper decision (Brans and De Smet, 2016). Therefore, in this paper both PROMETHEE I and II were applied.

Fig. 3. Stepwise procedure for PROMETHEE I and II (source: Behzadian et al., 2010).

first position: the most important; 3, last position: the least important). Ten Italian poultry scientists, 15 consumers and 15 farmers were interviewed and the mean values were reported. All stakeholders were informed about the meaning of each criterion to be sure that they properly classified the criteria. The results of such process are reported in Table 5 and were used to calculate the weights, which were then used in the MCDA.

Finally, in step 4, the positive and negative outranking flows were calculated for each alternative. The positive outranking flow (+(a)) represents the power of outranking of an alternative over all the others: the higher the +(a) the better the alternative (Brans and De Smet, 2016). The negative outranking flow (O -(a)) is a measure of the weakness of an alternative, and of how much it is outranked by all the others: the smaller the (a) the better the alternative (Brans and De Smet, 2016). PROMETHEE II goes to step 5 and calculates the net outranking flow (a), which is a measure of the balance between positive and negative flows. The higher the net flow, the better the alternative. However, in real-world appli- cations it is recommended to apply both PROMETHEE I and II: the complete ranking is easier to use, but the partial one can be useful to finalize a proper decision (Brans and De Smet, 2016). Therefore, in this paper both PROMETHEE I and II were applied.
35: <header>2.5. Multicriteria analysis </header>

To rank the three poultry systems, all the criteria described in section 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 were processed using PROMETHEE (Brans, 1982; Vincke and Brans, 1985), one of the most used outranking methods in the Multicriteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) family, applied to several fields of research (Behzadian et al., 2010). In particular, both PROMETHEE I (partial ranking) and PROMETHEE II (complete ranking) were applied, using Visual PROMETHEE software.

The structure preference of PROMETHEE is based on a pairwise comparison of two alternatives, for each criterion, that have to be maximized or minimized. The deviation between the evaluations of two alternatives on each criterion is considered: the larger the deviation, the larger the preference for one alternative.

Fig. 3 shows the stepwise procedure for applying PROMETHEE I and II (Behzadian et al., 2010), as applied also in this work. The first step is the determination of the deviation based on the pairwise comparison. In step 2, a preference function is applied to each criterion, to calculate the global preference index in the further Step 3. In our application, a linear preference function was applied to all the criteria except Labor Safety index, for which a level function was adopted. To estimate the global preference index, a weighting process is required. In outranking methods, as the PROMETHEE, weights represent the degree of importance of criteria, not considering the trade-off and scaling effects (de Almeida Filho e al., 2018). PROMETHEE assumes the ability of Decision Makers to weight the criteria appropriately (Behzadian et al., 2010; Macharis et al., 2004). Since several studies proposed the use of ordinal in- formation to establish weights values (de Almeida Filho e al., 2018), the weighting phase was processed by classifying the effects in priority categories.

|FARMERS|CONSUMERS|SCIENTISTS Environmental|Environmental|Environmental|Environmental Respiratory Inorganics|3|3|3 Climate change|3|1|1 Acidification/Eutrophication|3|2|2 Land use|2|2|2 Fossil fuel|3|1|1 Biodiversity index|3|3|1 Social|Social|Social|Social Labour safety index|1|2|2 Moving index|2|2|2 Stocking density|3|2|2 Time spending outdoor|2|1|1 Breast blister|1|2|1 Severe foot pad lesions|2|2|2 Landscape|3|1|3 Economics|Economics|Economics|Economics Net income per kilo|1|3|3 Feed conversion|1|3|2 Mortality rate|1|3|3 Tenderness|2|1|3 Fat content|2|1|1 n-3 fatty acids|2|1|1

maximize or minimize each criterion. Columns from 4 to 6 report the values for each criterion for the three systems. Considering the environmental performance, the intensive system performed best (i.e. lowest impact) for land use and fossil fuels, due to its high productivity. The combined system was the best considering climate change impact, acidification, respiration inorganics and biodiversity index, thanks to the avoided impact of
mowing and fertilization in the olive orchard. The free-range sys- tem had intermediate performances.

Under a social point of view, the intensive system was the worst, while the combined had the best performance, followed closely by the free-range system. From the economic perspective, the inten- sive system performed best in terms of feed conversion and mor- tality rate, while the combined and free-range systems were equally good for tenderness and fat content. The combined system performed best in terms of n-3 fatty acids and net income.

The MCDA application provided two different rankings for each group of stakeholders: a partial ranking by PROMETHEE I and a complete one using PROMETHEE II. Figs. 4-6 show the results provided by PROMETHEE I and II for each stakeholder group (farmers, consumers and scientists).

The upper part of each Figure shows the graphic results for both PROMETHEE I (thicker bars on the left and right sides) and PROMETHEE II (thin bar in the middle). For PROMETHEE I the thicker bar on the left represents the positive flow p+(a), while the bar on the right is the negative flow (a) whereas (a) represents the net value equal to the difference between the two flows. For both sides the higher the position of a system the better its ranking. The thin middle bars represent the net flow: in this bar the +0.5 and -0.5 values are marked for clarity. The numeric values of the positive, net and negative outranking flows are reported at the bottom of the figures.

From the farmers' perspective (Fig. 4) only the combined system had a positive global ranking, while the free-range system was close to zero, and the intensive system had a negative value. From the farmers' perspective, the three were relatively close, even though the combined system had higher values than both other systems, which had similar values. However, the intensive system had a very high value and, consequently, its global performance was the worst.
Also, for the consumers (Fig. 5), the only globally positive option was the combined. This resulted from the very good performance in terms of the negative flow and from the moderately good

performance in terms of the positive flow. Even though the positive flow of the combined systems was only moderately good, it was far better than for the other two systems. The intensive system had the worst performance in terms of both positive and negative flows.

According to scientists (Fig. 6), the best option was again the combined, while the free-range and the intensive systems had negative net outranking flow o(a), the intensive being more negative. Comparing the consumers with the scientists' perspec- tives, free-range and intensive systems had closer positive flow values under the scientist's scenario, while all systems had higher negative flow values.

A direct comparison of the three scenarios is presented in Fig. 7, using PROMETHEE II only. The comparison shows the presence of a distinct global ranking (combined > free-range > intensive) across the three scenarios.

41: The MCDA showed that the combined system performed best under all three stakeholders' scenarios, followed by free-range and then the intensive systems. The combined system was the only system with always positive net flow, while the free-range system always had close to zero values, and the intensive system always negative net flows. However, the strength of the combined system as the top solution was not the same across the different scenarios. In the farmers' scenario the combined system had a lower value for the Net flow, while the intensive system had a less negative value than in the other two scenarios. The positive result of the combined system under the farmers' scenario, but less strong in comparison to the others two, can be due to the great importance of several economic criteria in comparison to the relative importance of the animal welfare criteria in the farmers' perspective. Gocsik et al. (2015) studied the economic feasibility of production systems with different levels of animal welfare, and found that the broiler sector has the best chance of exploiting the added value of improved animal welfare, with a premium price. Therefore, in case

48: Although the way the results changed with the different sce- nario was consistent with the literature, we performed a sensitivity analysis in order to evaluate the stability of the results, and to investigate which criteria, if any, are more critical and able to change the results when their values are changed. We therefore performed a weight stability analysis, by testing the sensitivity of the ranking to changes in the weight attributed to the different criteria (Triantaphyllou and Sanchez, 1997). In particular, we looked

for rank reversal points, which are the levels of weight variation necessary to cause a rank reversal (i.e.a change in the relative po- sitions of two cases in a ranking). This phenomenon is typical of pairwise MCDA methods, such as PROMETHEE (Eppe and De SMet, 2017) and it has been widely reported and studied in numerous multicriteria decision aid methodologies (Wang and Luo, 2009). We performed the weight stability analysis using the visual stability interval, which shows how the final ranking varies as a function of the weights of a single criterion. Calculation of stability intervals helps to understand how much (in percentage) a weight has to change in order to reverse the final rank. The results are shown in Tables 7 and 8 for the weights which had rank reversal; all other weights had no rank reversal, meaning that the rank remained the same whatever the value of those weights.

Table 7 reports the stability interval between the free-range and intensive systems, which switch between the second and third positions, while Table 8 reports the stability interval between the intensive and combined systems, which switch between the second and first positions. Stability intervals are higher in Table 8, indi- cating that the ranking of the combined system, relative to the intensive system, is more stable than that of the free-range system, even for the four criteria that have rank reversal.

In all preference scenarios (farmers, consumers and scientists), 16 out of the 20 criteria did not have rank reversal, implying a strong stability of the ranking. In particular, none of the Social criteria had rank reversal. Only four criteria (Mortality Rate, Feed Conversion, Fossil Fuels and Land Use) had rank reversal, both between the free- range and intensive systems, and between the combined and intensive systems, but with relatively large stability intervals.

The four criteria with rank reversal were two environmental and two economic criteria. However, feed conversion efficiency is linked to both fossil fuel and land use: low feed conversion effi- ciency in broilers implies more land and fossil fuel to produce feed ingredients. The stability intervals of the two environmental criteria were lower than those of the economic criteria. Consumers and scientists had similar stability interval values for all four criteria, while the farmers had a lower value for fossil fuel and a higher value for mortality rate. The lower stability interval for land use and fossil fuels is coherent with the debate on the environ- mental impact of intensive and extensive broilers production. Fossil fuels represents the environmental hotspot of poultry production (Leinonen and Kyriazakis, 2016); therefore, rank reversal may happen because of the great importance of this criterion in the production context.
Stability interval for the intensive vs. the free-range system (expressed in percent- age). NRR indicates no rank reversal. Social criteria are not reported, since none of them had rank reversal.

Criteria|Farmers|Consumers|Scientists Environmental|Environmental|Environmental|Environmental Respiratory Inorganics|NRR|NRR|NRR Climate change|NRR|NRR|NRR Acidification/|NRR|NRR|NRR Eutrophication|NRR|NRR|NRR Land use|32.27%|40.07%|34.96% Fossil fuel|28.59%|42.93%|38.10% Biodiversity index|NRR|NRR|NRR Economics|NRR|NRR|NRR Net income per kilo|NRR|NRR|NRR Feed conversion|46.44%|49.92%|46.35% Mortality rate|29.80%|28.66%|24.10% Tenderness|NRR|NRR|NRR Fat content|NRR|NRR|NRR n-3 fatty acids|NRR|NRR|NRR
49: Although the way the results changed with the different sce- nario was consistent with the literature, we performed a sensitivity analysis in order to evaluate the stability of the results, and to investigate which criteria, if any, are more critical and able to change the results when their values are changed. We therefore performed a weight stability analysis, by testing the sensitivity of the ranking to changes in the weight attributed to the different criteria (Triantaphyllou and Sanchez, 1997). In particular, we looked

for rank reversal points, which are the levels of weight variation necessary to cause a rank reversal (i.e.a change in the relative po- sitions of two cases in a ranking). This phenomenon is typical of pairwise MCDA methods, such as PROMETHEE (Eppe and De SMet, 2017) and it has been widely reported and studied in numerous multicriteria decision aid methodologies (Wang and Luo, 2009). We performed the weight stability analysis using the visual stability interval, which shows how the final ranking varies as a function of the weights of a single criterion. Calculation of stability intervals helps to understand how much (in percentage) a weight has to change in order to reverse the final rank. The results are shown in Tables 7 and 8 for the weights which had rank reversal; all other weights had no rank reversal, meaning that the rank remained the same whatever the value of those weights.

Table 7 reports the stability interval between the free-range and intensive systems, which switch between the second and third positions, while Table 8 reports the stability interval between the intensive and combined systems, which switch between the second and first positions. Stability intervals are higher in Table 8, indi- cating that the ranking of the combined system, relative to the intensive system, is more stable than that of the free-range system, even for the four criteria that have rank reversal.

In all preference scenarios (farmers, consumers and scientists), 16 out of the 20 criteria did not have rank reversal, implying a strong stability of the ranking. In particular, none of the Social criteria had rank reversal. Only four criteria (Mortality Rate, Feed Conversion, Fossil Fuels and Land Use) had rank reversal, both between the free- range and intensive systems, and between the combined and intensive systems, but with relatively large stability intervals.

The four criteria with rank reversal were two environmental and two economic criteria. However, feed conversion efficiency is linked to both fossil fuel and land use: low feed conversion effi- ciency in broilers implies more land and fossil fuel to produce feed ingredients. The stability intervals of the two environmental criteria were lower than those of the economic criteria. Consumers and scientists had similar stability interval values for all four criteria, while the farmers had a lower value for fossil fuel and a higher value for mortality rate. The lower stability interval for land use and fossil fuels is coherent with the debate on the environ- mental impact of intensive and extensive broilers production. Fossil fuels represents the environmental hotspot of poultry production (Leinonen and Kyriazakis, 2016); therefore, rank reversal may happen because of the great importance of this criterion in the production context.
Stability interval for the intensive vs. the free-range system (expressed in percent- age). NRR indicates no rank reversal. Social criteria are not reported, since none of them had rank reversal.

Criteria|Farmers|Consumers|Scientists Environmental|Environmental|Environmental|Environmental Respiratory Inorganics|NRR|NRR|NRR Climate change|NRR|NRR|NRR Acidification/|NRR|NRR|NRR Eutrophication|NRR|NRR|NRR Land use|13.80%|13.59%|11.98% Fossil fuel|9.11%|17.72%|16.23% Biodiversity index|NRR|NRR|NRR Economic|NRR|NRR|NRR Net income per kilo|NRR|NRR|NRR Feed conversion|32.51%|26.20%|25.44% Mortality rate|21.28%|12.65%|10.49% Tenderness|NRR|NRR|NRR Fat content|NRR|NRR|NRR n-3 fatty acids|NRR|NRR|NRR

About the Land Use criterion, there is an issue with the way it is calculated in the LCA, which penalizes the free-range and combined systems because of the great area of land needed. As reported by Paolotti et al. (2016) free-range poultry enjoy better welfare and provide a higher meat quality but their environmental impact is often higher than in conventional systems, due to their lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use. The stability intervals between the intensive and the combined systems are considerably higher, in particular for Land Use and Feed Conversion. Therefore, according to the sensitivity analysis, it is clear that the first position of the combined system is stable and strong, given that 16 criteria have no rank reversal, and that even for the four criteria with rank reversal, the stability intervals are high.

On the other hand, the free-range system is less stable, at least for the four criteria with rank reversal, since the stability intervals are lower. The lower stability of the free-range system is consistent with previous findings since there is no agreement in the literature about whether free-range systems are more or less sustainable than intensive rearing systems, as already discussed. In fact, rank reversal occurs for those criteria with more controversial impact, i.e. land use or feed conversion.

56: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 57: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 58: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 59: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 60: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 61: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 62: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 63: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 64: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 65: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems.
