66: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 67: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 68: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems. 69: Meeting the human global need for food and for preserving nature is one of the biggest challenges of modern agriculture (Tilman et al., 2002). While extensive agriculture can be a solution to decrease agriculture's environmental impact, the high land de- mand due to the grazing and the low feed conversion efficiency of free range systems make this solution less competitive and sus- tainable. In this scenario, mixed crop-livestock farming systems appear to have a great potential for both food production and environmental preservation, and for achieving a higher standard of animal welfare.

In this study, using a multicriteria approach, we analyzed the sustainability of three different poultry production system: a con- ventional intensive indoor system, a free-range system and a free- range combined with an olive orchard. The multicriteria approach considered environmental, social and economic impacts of the three systems, under three different scenarios corresponding to the preferences of three groups of stakeholders (farmers, consumers, scientists). According to all three-perspectives, the best solution was the combined one. The sensitivity analysis showed that these

results were stable and could be inverted only by greatly changing the weights attributed to four out of 20 criteria, while the other 16 criteria did not have rank reversal. The better performance of the|12, 145-157. Corr, S.A., Gentle, M.J., McCorquodale, C.C., Bennett, D., 2003b. The effect of morphology on walking ability in the modern broiler: a gait analysis study. Anim. Welf. 12, 159-171. free-range combined system can be explained by the environ- mental and economic benefits achieved by combining free-range animals with the orchard. Such benefits could counteract the greater environmental impact of free-range birds, due to lower feed conversion efficiency and greater land use, compared to birds in intensive systems.
